items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.
Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: Correct answer: UK Design Council. “IDEO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: The real figure is 2 diamonds. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
